title: Localization of Chiral Electromagnetic Waves on Thick Axion Domain Walls
abstract: We analyze Maxwell theory coupled to an axion domain wall as a spectral boundary value problem. We find that a finite-width axion domain wall generically supports a localized normalizable chiral electromagnetic mode with linear, gapless dispersion. This mode arises from helicity-dependent coupling sourced by the axion gradient: one polarization experiences an effective attractive potential and forms a bound state, while the opposite polarization is repelled. The existence of this chiral surface photon is robust over a wide regime of wall structures and axion masses. Our result shows that axion domain walls generically support a localized chiral photon that has been missed in previous analyses.
summary: This paper theoretically investigates electromagnetic wave propagation near axion domain walls, finding that the wall structure inherently supports a robust, localized chiral photon mode. By extending spectral analysis from idealized delta-function limits to realistic finite thicknesses, the authors confirm this bound state exists even when previous adiabatic approximations fail. This suggests axion domain walls can act as natural waveguides for specific polarizations of light.
main_problem: The study analyzes the localization of chiral electromagnetic waves propagating along thick axion domain walls.
main_result: The analysis proves that such walls generically support a localized, normalizable chiral electromagnetic mode with linear, gapless dispersion, which was missed in prior analyses.
method: The problem is formulated as a spectral boundary value problem solved using the Lippmann-Schwinger framework and analyzing poles in the analytically continued resolvent of the wave operator.
model_or_system: The system involves Maxwell theory coupled to a background axion field $	heta(x)$ forming a domain wall. The analysis progresses from idealized delta-function walls to smooth, finite-width profiles.
key_observables: Localized chiral electromagnetic mode (chiral surface photon); Transmission ($T_{\pm}$) and reflection ($R_{\pm}$) coefficients; Dispersion relation ($\omega^2 = k_\parallel^2 / (1 + (\Delta	heta)^2/4)$).
important_parameters: Axion mass ($m_\phi$); Wall jump parameter ($\Delta	heta$); Frequency ($\omega$) relative to $m_\phi$; Wall thickness scale ($L$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on extending the spectral methods from the thin-wall limit to finite width, and in some steps, assumes small perturbations or monochromatic wave approximations.
paper_structure: The paper systematically builds the problem by first examining the ultrathin (delta-function) wall limit using scattering theory, then generalizing this robustly to finite thickness walls by analyzing the full spectral structure of the resulting 1+1 dimensional wave equation.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete example of how topological defects (domain walls) can act as natural waveguides or sources for exotic excitations (chiral photons), connecting high-energy physics concepts (axions) to condensed matter phenomena like surface modes and photonics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
